---
title: "Hierarchical structuring of weapon allometry and its evolutionary consequences."

authors:
  - name: Céline Ozkan
    affiliations:
      - ref: co
    corresponding: false
    email: celine.ozkan@etu.univ-lyon1.fr
    orcid: 
  - name: Clint D. Kelly
    affiliations:
      - ref: cdk
    corresponding: true
    email: kelly.clint@uqam.ca
    orcid: 0000-0002-0693-7211
    
affiliations:
  - id: cdk
    name: Département des Sciences biologiques, Université du Québec à Montréal, Montréal, Canada
  - id: co
    name: Université Claude Bernard Lyon 1, Lyon, France

filters:
  - authors-block
  - iagopinal/figure-table-formatter
  
file-import: true

format:
  docx:
    toc: false
    reference-doc: word-style-template.docx
bibliography: Bibliography.bib
csl: evolution.csl
---

```{r echo=FALSE}
knitr::read_chunk('scripts/2026_earwig_allometry.R')
```

```{r analysis, include=FALSE,echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE, results="hide"}
```

\newpage

# Abstract
Static allometries are often regarded as stable descriptors of morphological architecture, yet scaling relationships themselves can evolve and respond plastically to environmental variation. We quantified sex-specific and morph-specific weapon allometries in the European earwig (*Forficula auricularia*) under experimental manipulation of developmental diet and density, while estimating among-family variation in intercepts and scaling exponents. Forceps length scaled positively with body size in both sexes, but males exhibited steeper allometric slopes under favourable conditions. Nutritional stress reduced male scaling exponents towards female-like values, whereas increased developmental density amplified male slopes, indicating environmentally sensitive reaction norms of scaling. Female scaling relationships remained comparatively stable across environments. Within males, macrolabic and brachylabic morphs mainly differed in intercept rather than slope, showing divergence in baseline allocation within a largely shared scaling framework. Among families, intercepts and slopes covaried positively, suggesting that evolutionary change in mean exaggeration would likely alter size-dependent exaggeration as a correlated response. These findings demonstrate that weapon allometry is hierarchically structured across sex, morph, environment, and family levels, and that scaling exponents are evolvable components of exaggeration architecture. Instead of reflecting fixed scaling rules, static allometries function as multilevel trait complexes whose variance structure can influence the direction and magnitude of evolutionary responses to sexual selection.

\newpage

# Introduction
Static allometry describes how trait size scales with body size among individuals at the same developmental stage and provides a central framework for understanding the evolution of exaggerated morphology. Sexually selected weapons often exhibit positive allometry, such that larger individuals invest disproportionately more in trait size [@voje2016; @kodric-brown2006; @bonduriansky2007]. However, hyperallometry is not universal. When traits serve dual sexual and viability functions, or when functional and mechanical constraints limit disproportionate investment, isometry or hypoallometry may evolve instead [@bonduriansky2003; @eberhard2018]. Consequently, the scaling relationship itself reflects how developmental allocation, functional performance, and selection interact to shape trait architecture.

Static allometry can diverge due to changes in the scaling exponent or the intercept. Differences in scaling exponent reflect variation in how strongly a trait responds to body size, whereas intercept divergence reflects differences in baseline trait expression at a given body size. These components need not evolve together [@lande1980; @cheverud1982; @pelabon2014]. Sexual dimorphism, for example, may arise because males and females differ in scaling sensitivity, in baseline allocation, or in both. Similarly, alternative male morphs may diverge through elevation shifts while retaining similar scaling exponents, or through fundamentally different scaling architectures. Distinguishing between slope-based and intercept-based divergence is therefore essential for understanding how exaggerated traits evolve [@voje2016; @pelabon2014; @bonduriansky2007; @shingleton2013].

Species expressing alternative reproductive phenotypes provide particularly valuable models for examining the structure of allometry. In these species, males adopt discrete morphs that differ in weapon size, behaviour, and mating tactics [@gross1996; @shuster2003; @oliveira2008]. Because morphs share much of their genome and developmental environment, differences in scaling architecture can reveal how developmental pathways partition exaggeration within a single species. However, empirical analyses of morph-specific static allometry remain scarce, and even fewer studies have investigated how environmental factors influence both slopes and intercepts in polymorphic systems [@kochensparger2024].

The European earwig (*Forficula auricularia*) serves as a classic example of male weapon polymorphism. Males express discrete forceps morphs that vary considerably in size and curvature, commonly referred to as macrolabic and brachylabic forms [@simmons1996; @tomkins2005a]. Forceps function both as sexual weapons during male–male interactions and as defensive structures, making them traits with dual roles [@meunier2024; @kamimura2014]. Previous work has documented morph-specific differences in forceps exaggeration and scaling, suggesting that divergence occurs not only in absolute size but potentially in scaling architecture. Nonetheless, most studies have mainly compared  static slopes, with less focus directed toward intercept divergence, environmental sensitivity of scaling parameters, and variation among families.

Environmental conditions can influence developmental allocation and consequently reshape static allometries [@emlen1997; @frankino2005; @shingleton2007]. Nutritional stress may decreasse baseline investment in exaggerated traits, lowering  intercepts, whereas social factors such as rearing density could alter competitive allocation patterns. Effects of the environment on scaling exponents would suggest plasticity in the trait-to-body size relationship, whereas consistent slopes across environments would imply canalisation of the scaling architecture [@frankino2005; @shingleton2007; @nijhout2012]. Determining whether environmental variation modifies intercepts, slopes, or both is therefore crucial to evaluating the developmental flexibility of trait exaggeration.

Besides sex, morph, and environment, variation among families offers insights into the integration of scaling architecture. Intercept and slope may covary across lineages if developmental processes jointly regulate baseline allocation and scaling sensitivity [@lande1980; @pelabon2014; @hansen2008; @klingenberg2008]. A positive intercept–slope covariance indicates that families producing relatively larger weapons at average body size also have steeper scaling relationships, reflecting integrated exaggeration architecture [@bonduriansky2003; @pelabon2014; @hansen2008]. Conversely, independence between parameters suggests modularity or developmental decoupling of baseline exaggeration and scaling sensitivity.

Here, we quantify sex-specific and morph-specific static allometries of forceps length in *F. auricularia* under experimentally manipulated diet and density. Instead of viewing the scaling exponent as a fixed trait descriptor, we examine whether it functions as a condition-sensitive developmental parameter structured across biological levels. Specifically, we investigate whether sexual dimorphism in weapon exaggeration results from divergence in the scaling exponent, intercept, or both; whether macrolabic and brachylabic males differ mainly in baseline allocation or in the sensitivity of forceps growth to body size; and whether larval environmental conditions influence intercepts, slopes, or their covariance. Finally, by estimating variance among families in intercepts and scaling exponents, we determine whether baseline exaggeration and size sensitivity are developmentally integrated. Through integrating sex, morph, environment, and family components within a unified hierarchical framework, we assess whether static allometry is a stable architectural constant or an emergent property of layered growth regulation  [see also @simmons1996; @tomkins2005a; @kelly2025a].

# Methods

### Study species and experimental design
Females (n = 115) were hand-collected at night from vegetation in Parc Outremont (Montréal, QC, Canada) during September and October 2024. Individuals were transported to the laboratory at the Université du Québec à Montréal and placed individually in 90-mm Petri dishes filled with moist sand. Each dish was provisioned with a cotton-plugged water tube and TetraMin Tropical Flakes (United Pet Group, Inc., Germany), and each female was assigned a unique ID code. Earwigs were maintained in the dark at 10 °C in an incubator (Percival Scientific, Perry, IA, USA) to mimic overwintering conditions. Dishes were checked daily for egg laying, and food and water were replenished as needed. Food and water were removed at the time of egg laying (approximately early December), after which dishes were checked weekly. Starting January 2025, dishes were checked daily for egg hatching. The date of egg hatching was recorded; hatching occurred between 13 January and 29 January 2025, and all 115 females produced hatchlings.

For two weeks following hatching, families were provisioned with a water tube and food (TetraMin Tropical Flakes). This interval allowed nymphs to complete a moult and reach a size sufficient to withstand manual transfer to experimental dishes. To manipulate the developmental environment, we varied two ecological factors: nutritional condition (diet quality) and social density. Diet treatment consisted of two levels: a “good” diet (100% TetraMin Tropical Flakes), providing high nutritional quality, and a “poor” diet (75% TetraMin Tropical Flakes, 25% alpha-cellulose), providing reduced nutritional content. Density treatment consisted of three levels: low density (one individual per Petri dish), medium density (4 individuals), and high density (8 individuals). These treatments allowed us to examine both nutritional and social environmental effects on growth and morphology. We used a split-brood experimental design in which offspring were randomly assigned to diet and density treatments within maternal families to minimise confounding between maternal background and environmental treatment. Experimental dishes were maintained at 20 °C to mimic post-winter developmental conditions and contained a water tube, treatment-specific food, and a refuge (four small pieces of paper straw glued together). A total of 1,265 Petri dishes were prepared. Females (mothers) were euthanized by freezing at −20 °C after offspring had been assigned to treatments. Dishes were checked several times per week for mortality and sexual maturation, and food and water were replenished as needed.

### Morphological measurements
Once offspring eclosed to adulthood, they were removed from their Petri dish, the date recorded, and assigned a unique ID code. Pronotum width (a proxy for overall body size) and forceps length [following @vanlieshout2009] were photographed under a Leica S6D stereo microscope (Leica Microsystems Inc., Concord, ON, Canada) at 0.68× magnification using Enersight software, which automatically adds a scale bar to each image. Measurements were taken to the nearest 0.001 mm using Fiji image analysis software (Schindelin et al., 2012). All measurements were taken blind to treatment where possible. To assess repeatability, a subset of 30 individuals was measured twice. Intraclass correlation coefficients indicated high repeatability (pronotum width ICC = 0.94; forceps length ICC = 0.97). Log-transformed pronotum width was mean-centered before analysis to facilitate interpretation of intercepts and reduce collinearity among predictors.

### Data preparation
All analyses were conducted in R. We retained individuals from experimental dishes that met predefined density criteria to maintain experimental integrity of the density treatments (density 1: one survivor; density 4: ≥3 survivors; density 8: ≥6 survivors). Individuals lacking sex information were excluded. Forceps length and pronotum width were log-transformed before analysis to linearize allometric relationships and reduce heteroscedasticity. Log pronotum width was mean-centered to facilitate interpretation of intercepts and reduce collinearity in interaction models. GOOD diet and density 1 were treated as reference levels throughout. Female sex and brachylabic males served as reference categories where appropriate. Maternal identity was included as a grouping factor in all models to account for shared developmental background among siblings.

### Male morph classification
Male morphs (brachylabic and macrolabic) were classified using finite mixture modelling in the R package *mixsmsn* [@prates2013]. Consistent with the documented weapon dimorphism in earwigs, we modelled the distribution of raw male forceps length as a two-component skew-normal mixture. Forceps length was analysed without prior size correction so that morph classification reflects emergent weapon phenotype rather than residual variation after size correction. Models were estimated using the expectation–maximization algorithm with automatic initialization (maximum 1000 iterations). Model support for bimodality was evaluated using AIC and BIC. Posterior probabilities of component membership were obtained for each male, and individuals were assigned to morphs based on maximum posterior probability. All males were retained for subsequent analyses, thereby avoiding arbitrary trait thresholds and preserving variation near the morph boundary.

### Sex-specific allometry
To test whether males and females differed in forceps allometry and in the environmental sensitivity of scaling relationships, we fitted a hierarchical Gaussian model with log-transformed forceps length as the response. Fixed effects included mean-centered log pronotum width, sex, diet, density, and all lower-order interactions necessary to test sex differences in intercepts and scaling exponents across environmental treatments. Interactions between body size and environmental variables allowed scaling exponents to vary across conditions, and three-way interactions with sex tested whether environmental sensitivity of scaling differed between males and females. Maternal identity (n = 110 families) was included as a random intercept and random slope for body size, allowing families to vary in baseline forceps size and scaling exponent.

### Morph-specific allometry
To examine scaling architecture within males, we fitted a second model restricted to males. Log-transformed forceps length was modeled as a function of body size, morph (macrolabic versus brachylabic), diet, density, and all corresponding interactions. Body size × morph interactions tested for baseline slope divergence between morphs, and three-way interactions with environmental factors evaluated morph-specific slope plasticity. As morph classification was based on absolute forceps expression, this analysis tests whether emergent weapon phenotypes differ in scaling architecture without imposing size correction prior to morph assignment. Maternal identity (n = 104 families) was again included as a random intercept and slope for body size.

### Priors and model fitting
Models were fitted in a Bayesian framework [*brms*, @burkner2017] using multilevel Gaussian regression. Weakly informative priors were specified for fixed effects and variance components to regularize estimates while remaining non-restrictive on the log scale. Convergence and sampling adequacy were verified using standard diagnostics.

### Estimation of scaling exponents and plasticity
Condition-specific allometric slopes were derived from posterior draws as linear combinations of regression coefficients. Environmental plasticity in scaling was quantified as the deviation in slope from the reference condition (GOOD diet, density 1). We report posterior medians and 95% credible intervals for all slopes and slope differences. To evaluate biological relevance, we also calculated posterior probabilities that slope changes exceeded zero and that their absolute magnitude exceeded 0.1 on the log–log scale. All inference is based on posterior distributions rather than null-hypothesis significance testing.

# Results
### Sexual dimorphism in scaling relationships
Forceps length scaled positively with pronotum length in both sexes across all environmental treatments (@fig-one). Females exhibited a continuous distribution of weapon sizes across body sizes ($\bar{x}$ ± SE pronotum length = `r myround(summary.table.2[[3,6]],2)` ± `r myround(summary.table.2[[3,8]],3)`  mm; $\bar{x}$ ± SE forceps length = `r myround(summary.table.2[[3,2]],2)` ± `r myround(summary.table.2[[3,5]],3)` mm, n = `r round(summary.table.2[[3,4]],0)`), whereas males formed two discrete clusters corresponding to brachylabic ($\bar{x}$ ± SE pronotum length = `r myround(summary.table.2[[2,6]],2)` ± `r myround(summary.table.2[[2,8]],3)`  mm; $\bar{x}$ ± SE forceps length = `r myround(summary.table.2[[2,2]],3)` ± `r myround(summary.table.2[[2,5]],3)` mm, n = `r round(summary.table.2[[3,4]],0)`) and macrolabic morphs ($\bar{x}$ ± SE pronotum length = `r myround(summary.table.2[[1,6]],2)` ± `r myround(summary.table.2[[1,8]],3)` mm; $\bar{x}$ ± SE forceps length = `r myround(summary.table.2[[1,2]],2)` ± `r round(summary.table.2[[1,5]],3)`; n = `r round(summary.table.2[[1,4]],0)`) morphs but did not eliminate the clear separation between male morphs or the overall positive scaling relationship.

Forceps allometry differed markedly between the sexes (Table S1). Under good diet and low-density conditions (reference levels), females exhibited a positive but moderate scaling relationship between forceps length and body size (posterior mean slope = `r myround(summary(slopes_sex)[1,4],2)`, 95% CrI: `r myround(summary(slopes_sex)[1,5],2)`–`r myround(summary(slopes_sex)[1,6],2)`). Males showed substantially steeper scaling, as indicated by the positive interaction between body size and sex (logPc × sexmale = `r myround(tidy(mod_sex)[7,5],2)`, 95% CrI: `r myround(tidy(mod_sex)[7,7],2)`–`r myround(tidy(mod_sex)[7,8],2)`; Table S1). Under baseline conditions, the implied male slope was `r myround(summary(slopes_sex)[2,4],2)` (95% CrI: `r myround(summary(slopes_sex)[2,5],2)`–`r myround(summary(slopes_sex)[2,6],2)`). This difference is evident in the separation of predicted scaling relationships between sexes (@fig-two) and in posterior slope estimates (@fig-three).The posterior probability that male slopes exceeded female slopes was > `r myround(mean(beta_male_base > beta_female_base),4)`, indicating pronounced sexual dimorphism in scaling exponent. Sexes also differed strongly in intercept. At mean body size, males had larger forceps than females (sexmale = `r myround(tidy(mod_sex)[3,5],2)`, 95% CrI: `r myround(tidy(mod_sex)[3,7],2)`–`r myround(tidy(mod_sex)[3,8],2)`; Table S1), demonstrating clear size dimorphism independent of slope differences. Thus, sexual dimorphism in weapon exaggeration reflects divergence in both scaling exponent and mean trait size.

### Sex-specific environmental sensitivity
#### Intercept plasticity
Environmental effects on intercept were sex biased. Poor diet reduced forceps size at mean body size in females (dietPOOR = `r myround(tidy(mod_sex)[4,5],2)`, 95% CrI: `r myround(tidy(mod_sex)[4,7],2)`–`r myround(tidy(mod_sex)[4,8],2)`; Table S1). This reduction was substantially stronger in males, as indicated by the negative sex × diet interaction (sexmale:dietPOOR = `r myround(tidy(mod_sex)[9,5],2)`, 95% CrI: `r myround(tidy(mod_sex)[9,7],2)`–`r myround(tidy(mod_sex)[9,8],2)`; Table S1), yielding a total male intercept decline of approximately  `r myround(tidy(mod_sex)[4,5] + (tidy(mod_sex)[9,5]),2)` under poor diet.

Density effects on female intercepts were weak. In contrast, males exhibited positive intercept shifts at higher densities (sexmale:density4 = `r myround(tidy(mod_sex)[12,5],2)`; sexmale:density8 = `r myround(tidy(mod_sex)[13,5],2)`, both credible intervals excluding zero; Table S1). Thus, males were more environmentally sensitive than females in mean weapon size, showing both nutritional suppression and density-associated increases in exaggeration.

#### Slope plasticity
Female slopes were relatively stable across environments. The interaction between body size and diet was centered near zero (logPc:dietPOOR = `r myround(tidy(mod_sex)[8,5],2)`, 95% CrI: `r myround(tidy(mod_sex)[8,7],2)`–`r myround(tidy(mod_sex)[8,8],2)`; Table S1), and density interactions similarly overlapped zero. Female slopes were relatively stable across environments (@fig-four).

In contrast, males exhibited pronounced environment-dependent changes in scaling exponent (@fig-four). Under poor diet, the male slope declined substantially, as indicated by the negative three-way interaction (logPc × sexmale × dietPOOR = `r myround(tidy(mod_sex)[14,5],2)`, 95% CrI: `r myround(tidy(mod_sex)[14,7],2)`–`r myround(tidy(mod_sex)[14,8],2)`; Table S1). This corresponds to a reduction from approximately `r myround(tidy(mod_sex)[2,5] + tidy(mod_sex)[7,5], 2)` under good diet to approximately `r myround(tidy(mod_sex)[2,5] + tidy(mod_sex)[7,5]-tidy(mod_sex)[8,5]-tidy(mod_sex)[7,5], 2)` under poor diet. Posterior probability that male slope decreased under poor diet exceeded `r mean(beta_male_poor < beta_male_good)`, and the probability that the magnitude of change exceeded 0.1 was > `r myround(mean(abs(delta_male_diet) > 0.1),3)`. 

Density had the opposite effect, increasing male slopes at densities 4 and 8 (logPc × sexmale × density4 = `r myround(tidy(mod_sex)[15,5],2)`; logPc × sexmale × density8 = `r myround(tidy(mod_sex)[16,5],2)`; both credible intervals excluding zero). Posterior probabilities that male slope increases exceeded 0.1 were > `r mean(abs(delta_male_density4) > 0.1)`. Across treatments, male slope plasticity exceeded female slope plasticity with high probability (> `r myround(mean(abs(delta_male_diet) > abs(delta_female_diet)),3)`).

Together, these results demonstrate pronounced male-specific plasticity in scaling exponent, whereas female scaling relationships appear comparatively canalized.

### Morph differences among males
Given the strong male-specific plasticity, we next examined scaling architecture within males.

#### Baseline morph differences
Under good diet and low-density conditions, brachylabic males e xhibited a slope of `r myround(tidy(mod_morph)[2,5],2)` (95% CrI: `r myround(tidy(mod_morph)[2,7],2)`–`r myround(tidy(mod_morph)[2,8],2)`; Table S2). Macrolabic males showed a nearly identical baseline slope (logPc:morphmacrolabic = `r myround(tidy(mod_morph)[2,5]+tidy(mod_morph)[7,5],2)` (95% CrI: `r myround(tidy(mod_morph)[7,7],2)`–`r myround(tidy(mod_morph)[7,8],2)`; Table S2), with posterior probability of a positive difference equal to `r mean(delta_baseline > 0)`. Thus, there was little evidence of baseline slope divergence between morphs.Baseline slope similarity between morphs is evident in predicted scaling relationships (@fig-two) and posterior slope estimates (@fig-three).

Morphs differed strongly in intercept. At mean body size, macrolabic males had substantially larger forceps than brachylabic males (macrolabic = `r myround(tidy(mod_morph)[3,5],2)`, 95% CrI: `r myround(tidy(mod_morph)[3,7],2)`–`r myround(tidy(mod_morph)[3,8],2)`; Table S2). Morph divergence in weapon size is therefore primarily elevation-based rather than slope-based.

#### Morph-specific environmental sensitivity
Diet had little effect on intercept in brachylabic males (dietPOOR = `r myround(tidy(mod_morph)[4,5],2)`, 95% CrI: `r myround(tidy(mod_morph)[4,7],2)`–`r myround(tidy(mod_morph)[4,8],2)`; Table S2), but reduced intercept in macrolabic males (morphmacrolabic:dietPOOR = `r myround(tidy(mod_morph)[9,5],2)`, 95% CrI: `r myround(tidy(mod_morph)[9,7],2)`–`r myround(tidy(mod_morph)[9,8],2)`; Table S2). Nutritional stress therefore disproportionately reduced weapon size in the exaggerated morph.

Both morphs showed some tendency toward reduced scaling exponents under poor diet. In brachylabic males, slope decline was modest (logPc:dietPOOR = `r myround(tidy(mod_morph)[8,5],2)`, 95% CrI: `r myround(tidy(mod_morph)[8,7],2)`–`r myround(tidy(mod_morph)[8,8],2)`; Table S2). In macrolabic males, the additional diet-dependent slope change in macrolabic males was positive but highly uncertain (logPc:morphmacrolabic:dietPOOR = `r myround(tidy(mod_morph)[14,5],2)`, 95% CrI: `r myround(tidy(mod_morph)[14,7],2)`–`r myround(tidy(mod_morph)[14,8],2)`; Table S2). Posterior contrasts indicated that diet-induced slope plasticity tended to be greater in macrolabic than brachylabic males (posterior probability ≈ `r myround(mean(abs(delta_macro_diet) > abs(delta_brachy_diet)),2)`), although credible intervals were broad.

Density-induced slope changes were comparatively weak and uncertain in both morphs, and there was little evidence that density altered scaling exponents differently between morphs. Morph-specific changes in allometric slope relative to baseline conditions are shown in @fig-five.

Overall, within males, morph differentiation primarily reflects intercept shifts rather than fundamentally distinct scaling exponents.

### Among-family variation
At the sex level, there was detectable among-maternal-family variance in scaling exponent (sd(logPc) = `r myround(tidy(mod_sex)[18,5],2)`, 95% CrI: `r myround(tidy(mod_sex)[18,7],2)`–`r myround(tidy(mod_sex)[18,8],2)`) and smaller variance in intercept (sd(Intercept) = `r myround(tidy(mod_sex)[17,5],2)`). The intercept–slope correlation was weak and uncertain (r = `r myround(tidy(mod_sex)[19,5],2)`, CrI overlapping zero), suggesting limited evidence for integrated family-level variation across sexes (Table S2). 

Within males, family-level variation in slope was larger (sd(logPc) = `r myround(tidy(mod_morph)[18,5],2)`, 95% CrI: `r myround(tidy(mod_morph)[18,7],2)`–`r myround(tidy(mod_morph)[18,8],2)`). Intercepts and slopes were positively correlated among families (r = `r myround(tidy(mod_morph)[19,5],2)`, 95% CrI: `r myround(tidy(mod_morph)[19,7],2)`–`r myround(tidy(mod_morph)[19,8],2)`), indicating that broods producing sons with larger forceps at mean body size also tended to exhibit steeper scaling relationships.

Because females were field-collected and potentially multiply mated, these variance components reflect combined genetic and shared environmental effects. Nonetheless, the positive covariance demonstrates integrated brood-level variation in exaggeration architecture.

### Summary
Weapon allometry exhibits a hierarchical structure. Sexual dimorphism is characterized by steeper and more environmentally sensitive scaling in males relative to females. In males, morphological divergence is primarily intercept-based, with macrolabic males consistently expressing larger weapons and greater sensitivity to nutritional stress at the intercept. Slope plasticity is pronounced at the sex level and detectable, though weaker and more uncertain, within males, while baseline slopes are similar across morphs. Finally, male scaling intercepts and exponents covary positively among maternal families, indicating integrated clutch-level variation in exaggeration architecture.

# Discussion
Static allometries are often regrdarded as stable descriptors of morphological architecture [@huxley1932; @voje2016; @gould1966]. Our findings challenge this perspective by demonstrating that the scaling exponent itself can act as a condition-dependent developmental trait structured across biological levels. In this earwig, weapon exaggeration is not defined by a single static slope, but by reaction norms of scaling that differ between sexes, vary across environments, and covary among families. Males expressed steep positive allometry under favourable conditions, consistent with expectations for sexually selected weapons [@kodric-brown2006; @bonduriansky2007], yet their scaling exponents declined toward female-like values under nutritional stress and steepened under increased density. Females, by contrast, exhibited comparatively stable scaling relationships across environments. These results suggest that the allometric slope is not a fixed architectural constant but an environmentally responsive parameter of growth regulation [@shingleton2007; @frankino2005; @nijhout2012], expanding theory on condition dependence beyond mean trait expression to the structure of scaling itself [@rowe1996; @cotton2004; @tomkins2004; @pelabon2014]. Instead of reflecting fixed scaling rules, static allometries emerge as layered outcomes shaped by sex, environment, and family-level variation.

The evolution of exaggerated traits is often understood through the perspectives of condition dependence and genic capture theory [@rowe1996; @cotton2004; @tomkins2004], which predicts heightened environmental sensitivity of sexually selected characters. Most empirical work has focused on mean trait expression, yet theory does not restrict condition dependence to shifts in intercepts. If growth regulation is condition sensitive, the scaling exponent itself should also respond to environmental inputs [@shingleton2007; @frankino2005; @nijhout2012; @emlen1997]. Our findings provide direct evidence for such slope plasticity in a weapon trait. The size–trait relationship becomes less steep under poor diet, indicating reduced exaggeration among larger males. Under higher density, larger males exaggerate more strongly, making the slope steeper. Because the scaling exponent controls how disproportionately trait size increases with body size, plasticity in slope directly influences the strength of size-dependent exaggeration and thus the opportunity for sexual selection to favour larger individuals. Therefore, male weapon exaggeration is influenced not only by overall resource allocation but also by environment-dependent restructuring of the size–trait relationship. If selection favours greater exaggeration in larger males, it may act not just on the average trait expression but also on the slope of the size–trait reaction norm itself. In this perspective, the scaling exponent becomes a target of selection rather than a fixed constraint.

The lack of clear slope divergence between brachylabic and macrolabic males refines the understanding of morphological differentiation. Although macrolabic males have considerably larger forceps at a given body size, their scaling exponent remains similar to that of brachylabics. Therefore, morphological divergence is mainly due to elevation rather than slope differences. Distinguishing between changes in intercept and slope is important because these parameters indicate different aspects of growth regulation: slope reflects how trait size responds to body size, while intercept indicates baseline allocation at a given body size [@bonduriansky2003; @pelabon2014]. Elevation shifts without major changes in the exponent imply alteration in the allocation magnitude within a generally shared scaling framework [@klingenberg2008; @lande1980; @hansen2008]. This pattern broadly aligns with environmental threshold models of conditional strategy evolution  [@tomkins2007; @hazel1990; @buzatto2015], where discrete phenotypes develop once condition exceeds a developmental threshold, while underlying size-dependent growth relationships remain partly conserved. Developmental models demonstrate how such thresholds can originate from nonlinear regulation of growth pathways [@emlen2000]. In these systems, morph determination may change mean trait expression without fundamentally restructuring the scaling architecture. Our results therefore imply that the earwig weapon dimorphism results from divergence in baseline allocation rather than from the evolution of separate allometric programmes.

An additional aspect of our data concerns the developmental basis of morph determination. Although macrolabic males generally have significantly larger forceps, there is considerable overlap in body size between morphs, with some small males showing the macrolabic phenotype and many large males remaining brachylabic (see @fig-one). If morph expression was determined solely by a strict body-size threshold, a clearer transition would be expected, with large males consistently displaying the exaggerated morph. The overlap observed thus indicates that adult body size alone is not enough to predict morph identity. Previous research has debated whether the forceps dimorphism in *F. auricularia* results from a purely status-dependent threshold mechanism or whether genetic factors also influence morph determination [@tomkins1999]. That study demonstrated both environmental and genetic factors influencing morph expression, raising the possibility that morph identity is not solely determined by condition or size. Our findings support this perspective. The notable intercept differences between morphs, along with generally similar scaling exponents, indicate that macrolabic and brachylabic males may follow distinct developmental pathways that share a common scaling structure but differ in their baseline investment in forceps length [see also @kelly2024; @kelly2025a]. Although we cannot directly estimate the heritability of morph identity, the overlap in body size between morphs, along with detectable among-family variation in scaling parameters, suggests that the developmental regulation of forceps dimorphism is unlikely to be explained solely by a simple size-based switch.

This hierarchical structure—sex-specific slope plasticity combined with morph-specific intercept divergence—indicates that exaggeration may evolve through layered modifications of scaling architecture rather than through uniform shifts in trait expression. Sex-limited expression can separate male scaling from female scaling, allowing selection to modify the male size–trait reaction norm without restricting female growth pathways. Among males, morph differentiation may change allocation levels while leaving baseline exponent dynamics largely unchanged, enabling exaggeration to increase via elevation shifts without comprehensive restructuring of scaling relationships. This hierarchical partitioning of variance suggests that selection can act at multiple biological levels simultaneously, modifying the slope at the sex level, the intercept at the morph level, and the covariance structure at the family level. By distributing variance across these levels, the scaling architecture may support evolutionary divergence while maintaining integration within each level, shaping the direction and tempo of responses to sexual selection [@bonduriansky2003; @lande1980; @pelabon2014].

The positive covariance between intercept and slope among maternal families within males offers insight into the integration of scaling components and their potential evolvability in weapon exaggeration. Families producing sons with relatively larger forceps at average body size also tend to exhibit steeper scaling exponents, indicating coordinated variation in baseline allocation and size sensitivity. Intercept and slope are theoretically separable elements of static allometry, and their covariance structure determines how scaling relationships can respond to selection [@bonduriansky2003; @pelabon2014; @hansen2008]. Positive intercept–slope covariance implies that selection favoring increased exaggeration at a given body size may simultaneously increase scaling exponent, thereby amplifying disproportionate investment in larger individuals. Because intercept and slope covary positively among families, evolutionary change in mean exaggeration would be expected to shift the degree of size-dependent exaggeration simultaneously, shaping the trajectory of multivariate evolutionary response. Such integration could accelerate evolutionary divergence in exaggeration intensity if genetic variance underlies these clutch-level differences [@lande1980; @pelabon2014]. Although our family effects combine genetic and shared environmental components, the presence of structured covariance indicates that scaling architecture itself varies among lineages rather than being developmentally fixed. This integrated variation may offer structured differences upon which selection could act, supporting the view that static allometry is likely to be evolvable. Static allometry is thus not only a descriptive relationship but also a complex trait with structured variance at multiple levels, suggesting that scaling relationships can evolve in response to selection. Our findings show that predicting evolutionary responses to sexual selection requires measuring not only the condition dependence of mean trait expression but also the environmental sensitivity of scaling exponents and their covariance structure. Without accounting for these aspects, evolutionary models of exaggeration may miss important dimensions of the response.

In summary, these results advance a view of static allometry as an evolvable and environmentally responsive trait complex [@pelabon2014]. Instead of viewing the scaling exponent as a fixed aspect of morphological design, our findings suggest it is a condition-sensitive reaction norm that can differ between sexes and vary among families. Sexual dimorphism in scaling thus indicates not only differences in trait size but also how growth responds to environmental factors. In males, different morphs mainly reflect shifts in the baseline of allocation, while the fundamental scaling exponent remains largely consistent. The positive relationship between the intercept and slope further shows that baseline exaggeration and scaling sensitivity are developmentally connected, offering coordinated variation that selection can act upon. Overall, these patterns imply that the evolution of exaggerated weapons is driven not just by changes in average trait size but by a hierarchical modification of growth regulation across sexes, morphs, and lineages. More broadly, our results highlight that the evolutionary dynamics of exaggeration depend as much on the plasticity and integration of scaling relationships as on the extent of exaggeration itself.

# Data availability

Data are available through the OSF repository at 10.17605/OSF.IO/MFJ8E.

# Funding

The study was funded by a Discovery Grant from the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada (NSERC) to CDK.

# Conflict of Interest

We declare no conflicts of interest.

# Acknowledgements

We thank Andreanne Nault for her help in the lab.

\newpage

# References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::

\newpage

## Figures
::: {#fig-one fig-cap="Static allometries of forceps length as a function of pronotum length across density treatments (low, medium, high). Females (top row) and males (bottom row) are shown separately. In males, brachylabic (solid points) and macrolabic (open points) morphs are distinguished, with diet treatments indicated by shading. Males form two discrete clusters corresponding to morphotype, with substantial overlap in body size between morphs. Environmental treatments shift forceps length within morphs but do not eliminate size overlap. The dashed horizontal line in male panels indicates the approximate threshold commonly used to distinguish morphs based on forceps length."}
![](figure_1.jpg)
:::

::: {#fig-two fig-cap="Predicted population-level relationships between log-transformed forceps length and log-transformed pronotum width across diet (GOOD vs POOR) and rearing density (1, 4, or 8 individuals per container). Panels show females (left), brachylabic males (center), and macrolabic males (right). Solid, dashed, and dotted lines correspond to density treatments; color indicates diet. Fitted lines represent posterior medians from Bayesian multilevel models excluding random family effects; grey points show raw data. Female slopes are relatively stable across environments, whereas male slopes exhibit pronounced environmental sensitivity. Morph divergence in males is primarily intercept-based, with macrolabic males exhibiting consistently larger forceps at a given body size and stronger slope reductions under poor diet."}
![](figure_2.jpg)
:::

\newpage

::: {#fig-three fig-cap="Points represent posterior medians and horizontal bars indicate 95% credible intervals for slopes relating log forceps length to log pronotum width. Treatments are grouped by diet (good vs poor) and density (low, medium, or high). The vertical dotted line indicates zero slope. Female slopes cluster tightly and show little environmental sensitivity. In contrast, male slopes vary substantially across environments, with macrolabic males exhibiting a higher posterior probability of substantial slope reduction under poor diet than brachylabic males. These estimates are derived from Bayesian multilevel models incorporating among-family variance."}
![](figure_3.jpg)
:::

::: {#fig-four fig-cap="Posterior medians (points) and 95% credible intervals (horizontal bars) show changes in the allometric slope (Δβ) of log forceps length on log pronotum width relative to the baseline condition (good diet, low density). The vertical dashed line indicates no change in slope; dotted lines denote a biologically meaningful threshold of |Δβ| = 0.1. Females exhibit little evidence of slope plasticity across environments. In contrast, males show pronounced environmental sensitivity, with strong reductions in scaling exponent under poor diet and substantial increases at elevated densities. Estimates are derived from the sex-specific multilevel model including sex × environment × body size interactions."}
![](figure_4.jpg)
:::

::: {#fig-five fig-cap="Posterior medians and 95% credible intervals show changes in the allometric slope (Δβ) relative to brachylabic males under good diet and low density. The vertical dashed line indicates no change in slope; dotted lines denote |Δβ| = 0.1. Both morphs exhibit environmentally induced slope variation, but credible intervals broadly overlap, providing limited evidence for strong morph-specific divergence in scaling plasticity. Morph differentiation in weapon exaggeration therefore reflects primarily intercept shifts rather than fundamentally distinct scaling exponents. Estimates are derived from the male-only multilevel model including morph × environment × body size interactions."}
![](figure_5.jpg)
:::
